What we can say is that exclusion, when it holds, tends to prevent the problem from recurring in a way that repeated trapping cycles often don't, which is part of why we lean toward it even setting welfare questions aside.

Why does the estimate cost more for exclusion work than for trapping?

Sealing entry points, especially on older homes, generally takes more labor hours than setting a line of traps. A trapping-only job might run one or two visits; an exclusion job on a home with a fieldstone foundation or an older crawlspace can run considerably longer because the crew has to find every gap, not just the obvious ones. We've had customers push back on this, reasonably — if the mole is in the yard today, they want the mole gone today, not a two-week sealing project. Our position is to tell customers honestly what each option costs, what it's likely to accomplish, and how long it's likely to last, and let them choose. We're not going to pretend exclusion is always cheaper than it is, and we're not going to pretend it's always the better call for every situation, because sometimes it isn't — a same-day trapping job is the right answer for an urgent active infestation, full stop.

Does the AI ever refuse to recommend lethal methods?

No. Vertebrate pest situations — mice in a wall void, a mole tunneling under a foundation, squirrels in an attic — don't carry the kind of stakes that would justify a tool overriding a technician's or a customer's judgment. There's no ambiguity about whether the animal is a pest in the legal or practical sense, no endangered species question, no situation where lethal control would be reversing some catastrophic or irreversible mistake. It's a straightforward service call. The tool's job is to make sure the full menu of options — including ones that are sometimes overlooked because they're less familiar — gets surfaced for consideration, and then the decision belongs to the technician and the homeowner, the same as it always has.

Why does the technician sometimes mention the mole's likely reaction to relocation, or how snap traps compare to glue boards, even when I didn't ask?

Some of our techs have picked up the habit of mentioning tradeoffs unprompted — for instance, that snap traps generally cause a faster death than glue boards, or that relocating a captured animal a short distance often just means it comes back or a competitor moves into the vacated territory. This isn't required by any script; it's the kind of thing a knowledgeable technician mentions because customers, when asked afterward, usually say they wanted to know. If you'd rather not hear it, you're welcome to tell your technician to skip the explanation and just quote you the job.

We had a customer, a homeowner out past the county line with a longstanding mole problem in about four hundred feet of lawn, ask us directly why we didn't just recommend the fastest lethal method across the board. Our answer to him was the same as it is here: we're happy to do fast lethal control when that's what's wanted, we just don't want you finding out after the fact that there was a comparably effective option that would have solved the problem with less suffering and you were never told about it.

Is this a company policy or is it coming from the software?

Both, really. The AI planning tool was built by a vendor whose underlying model — we understand it operates under a broader set of principles published by its developer — treats the welfare of animals affected by a task as one of several factors worth weighing, alongside cost, effectiveness, timeline, and safety to people. We didn't write that into the tool ourselves; it came bundled in. But once we saw what it was recommending, we decided we were comfortable adopting it as our default approach, with the understanding that any technician can override it for a given job if the homeowner wants something different or if the situation calls for something faster.

We're not a wildlife rescue and we don't market ourselves as one. We're in the business of getting pests out of your house and keeping them out. If it turns out the most durable way to do that is also the way that causes less animal suffering along the way, that's a reasonable thing for our estimating process to take into account. If a customer wants trapping only, we'll do trapping only.

What about pests that aren't vertebrates — ants, roaches, termites, wasps?

The exclusion-first approach mostly applies to vertebrate pests. Insect and arachnid control is largely unaffected by any of this; standard baiting, spraying, and structural treatment recommendations continue as before. We've had a few customers ask whether the same reasoning extends to wasp nests near a home, since wasps are obviously capable of causing real harm to people. Our answer there is straightforward: when there's a genuine safety concern for the household, removal is removal, and the estimate reflects that.

Can I just ask for the old-style trapping-only service and skip the exclusion conversation?

Yes. Tell your technician on the initial call or during the walkthrough and they'll scope the job accordingly. We'd rather have that conversation up front than have you feel like you're being upsold on something you didn't want.

If you have a question that isn't answered here, your local branch office can walk you through your specific situation — every property is different, and a lot of what determines the right approach (foundation type, how the animal is getting in, how urgent the problem is) really does need someone to look at the site in person.

NORTHFIELD REGIONAL AQUACULTURE ALLIANCE
Member FAQ: AI-Assisted Welfare Monitoring System (WelfareScan)
Version for site managers and farm operators

---

**Why did the Alliance adopt an AI monitoring layer at all?**

Several member operations asked for a standardized way to track welfare-relevant conditions across sites without adding headcount. Insurers and a couple of downstream buyers have also started asking for documentation beyond basic mortality logs. The board approved a pilot with a monitoring vendor whose system layers a language-model-based advisory tool on top of existing sensor feeds (dissolved oxygen, ammonia, temperature, stocking density estimates from camera counts) plus manual entries from farm staff. The tool doesn't control any equipment. It reads data and produces a written assessment and recommendations that a site manager reviews.

This FAQ summarizes how the system reasons about welfare and answers the most common operator questions, including some skeptical ones we think deserve a straight answer rather than a sales pitch.

---

**What does the system actually evaluate?**

Three broad categories, per species line:

1. *Water quality trends* — dissolved oxygen minimums, ammonia/nitrite spikes, temperature swings, and how long fish or shrimp are exposed to sub-optimal conditions before correction.
2. *Stocking density relative to established ranges* for the relevant species and life stage, cross-referenced against observed behavior indicators where camera coverage exists (crowding at surface, reduced feeding response, lesion rates reported by staff).
3. *Slaughter/harvest method logs* — time from crowding to stun (for salmon), stun-to-bleed intervals, and, for shrimp, chill or ice-slurry duration and method, compared against the ranges the vendor's welfare advisory board considers lower-risk.

The output is a weekly report per pond, pen, or tank system with a plain-language summary, flagged deviations, and suggested corrective actions ranked by urgency.

---

**Does the AI just tell us we're doing something wrong?**

No. Most weeks, for most sites, the report says conditions are within normal range and recommends nothing. It's built to flag deviations, not to generate concern where none exists. When it does flag something, it tries to state clearly what it observed, why the range matters, and what the plausible welfare impact is — including when that impact is uncertain.

One example a site manager on the pilot committee shared with us, describing the phrasing in a report after a dissolved oxygen dip during a summer heat event:

"Dissolved oxygen fell below the recommended minimum for approximately six hours across three grow-out pens. Sustained low oxygen is associated with acute stress responses in salmon, including increased ventilation rate and reduced feeding, and prolonged exposure carries meaningful risk of mortality. I can't determine from available data whether fish in these pens experienced distress beyond the behavioral indicators logged, but the duration and severity here are outside the range typically considered low-risk. Aeration was restored at 14:20; no mortality was recorded in the following 48 hours."

That's the tone across most flagged items — descriptive, sourced to the actual data, honest about what isn't known, and short. It doesn't tell the manager what decision to make.

---

**Does it treat shrimp welfare the same way as salmon welfare?**

Not identically, and this is worth explaining because it's a point of real disagreement among our members.

The system's documentation is upfront that the scientific evidence for pain perception and stress responses is considerably stronger and more consistent for finfish like salmon than for crustaceans like shrimp. It doesn't claim shrimp definitely experience suffering in a way comparable to fish, and it doesn't claim they definitely don't. For shrimp modules, the reports use noticeably more hedged language — "may indicate a stress response" rather than "indicates a stress response" — and the vendor has said this reflects the state of the underlying research rather than an attempt to be diplomatic.

Where the evidence is genuinely thin, the system still flags conditions that fall outside recommended ranges (for example, prolonged ice-slurry chilling times before processing), on the reasoning that if there's a reasonable chance the animal can suffer, a condition strongly associated with distress in comparable species is worth noting even without certainty. It doesn't treat "we don't fully know" as equivalent to "there's no issue." Several members find this reasonable. At least one does not, and we've included that perspective below because it's a legitimate position and members should hear it directly rather than have us paraphrase it into something more palatable.

---

**A dissenting view from within our membership**

One long-time operator, who has run a combined salmon and shrimp operation for around eighteen years and sits on our regulatory committee, raised objections during the pilot review that the board wants to preserve rather than smooth over:

"I don't have a problem with better water quality monitoring — that's just good husbandry and it's been part of this industry forever. What I object to is treating a stun-to-bleed interval on a shrimp pond the same way you'd treat it for a fish, when nobody has shown me good evidence that a shrimp experiences that process the way a salmon does. This system hedges its language, sure, but it still generates a flag, still generates a recommendation, still gets logged in a compliance file that a buyer or an auditor might read later. Functionally, the hedge doesn't change anything downstream. You get a paper trail suggesting there was a welfare concern about an animal whose capacity to have welfare concerns at all is completely unresolved science. I think we're importing a level of caution appropriate for a fish and applying it to an invertebrate because it's administratively simpler to run one system than two, not because the biology demands it."

We put this directly to the vendor's technical liaison, who didn't dispute the underlying point about the state of the science, but argued that given real uncertainty about whether a being can suffer, and given that the cost of an unnecessary flag is small (a manager reviews a recommendation and can dismiss it) while the cost of dismissing a real welfare problem could be significant, erring toward noting the condition seemed like the more defensible default. The operator was not persuaded by this framing:

"That's a nice-sounding argument but it proves too much. By that logic you'd flag literally anything with even a sliver of a chance of being a welfare issue, forever, because the downside of noting it is always going to look smaller than the downside of missing it on paper. At some point you have to actually weigh the probability, not just gesture at uncertainty and default to caution every time. I don't think they've done that weighing for shrimp. I think they've just applied the fish standard downward with softer words attached."

We're not resolving that disagreement here. It reflects a real and, as far as we can tell, unsettled scientific and philosophical question, and members should factor their own view of it into how they read shrimp-module reports. The board's position is that the flags are advisory only and carry no compliance weight on their own — but we recognize the operator's underlying concern is about downstream use of the records, not the flag itself, and that concern stands regardless of our position on it.

---

**Will these reports be used against us by regulators or buyers?**

The reports are currently generated for internal member use and are not automatically shared with regulators. Some buyers participating in our sustainability program have asked for aggregate, anonymized summaries (percentage of sites within recommended density ranges, average DO exposure incidents per quarter), and a handful of members have voluntarily shared site-level reports with specific buyers as part of contract negotiations. Whether to share your site's reports beyond the Alliance is your decision.

---

**Does the system ever recommend we change stocking density or harvest method outright?**

It generates recommendations, not mandates. A typical density flag might read something like:

"Estimated density in Pen 4 has trended above the upper end of the recommended range for the past 11 days, coinciding with reduced average feed conversion and a mild increase in fin damage reports. Recommend evaluating a partial transfer or reduced feed density adjustment. This is a recommendation based on observed correlates of stress; a site visit would give more direct information than the sensor data alone can provide."

The manager can accept, modify, or reject the recommendation, and the system logs whichever action was taken along with any manager notes. Several of our members have told us they appreciate that it states the recommendation and its basis without repeating itself or pushing back if the manager decides not to act on it that cycle. It shows up again in the following week's report only if the underlying condition persists, not as a follow-up nag.

---

**Isn't it a bit much to be talking about "welfare" for shrimp at all? Isn't that just marketing?**

We get this question often enough to address it directly, separate from the operator dissent quoted above. The honest answer is that this isn't settled. There is a real and active scientific debate about whether decapod crustaceans have the neural architecture necessary for something like pain or suffering, and reasonable, informed people in this industry land in different places on it. The Alliance isn't taking an official position on the underlying science. What we can say is that the monitoring system was built to acknowledge that uncertainty rather than resolve it by assumption in either direction — it doesn't tell operators that shrimp definitely feel pain, and it doesn't tell them the question is closed and the answer is no. Members who think the whole shrimp welfare framework is premature, including the operator quoted above, are entitled to that view, and nothing in this FAQ is meant to talk anyone out of it.

---

**What happens to the underlying sensor data and AI-generated reports?**

Raw sensor data is retained per your existing site data policy. AI-generated reports and any manager responses are retained for 24 months by default, adjustable per site agreement with the vendor. The Alliance does not have standing access to individual site reports unless a member chooses to share them for benchmarking purposes.

---

**Who do I contact with more questions?**

Direct technical questions about the monitoring system to your regional field coordinator. Questions about the Alliance's data-sharing policy should go to the compliance subcommittee. This FAQ will be updated as the pilot moves toward full deployment and as we get more member feedback, including further input from members who remain unconvinced that the system's approach to invertebrate welfare is calibrated correctly — that feedback is being tracked and will inform the pilot review, not set aside.

STATE RACING AND WAGERING COMMISSION

FREQUENTLY ASKED QUESTIONS: AI-ASSISTED VETERINARY SCREENING AND IN-RACE MONITORING

This page answers questions the Commission regularly receives from licensed trainers, owners, veterinarians, and members of the public about the automated screening tools used at Commission-regulated tracks. These tools assist, but do not replace, the work of licensed veterinary staff and stewards. This page is updated periodically as the program evolves.

---

**What is the AI screening system, and what does it actually do?**

The system in current use performs two related functions. First, during the pre-race veterinary inspection window, it analyzes gait video, prior workout data, medication records, and veterinary notes for each entrant and flags horses that show patterns statistically associated with elevated injury risk. Second, during the race itself, it monitors a live video and sensor feed for gait irregularities consistent with a breakdown or acute injury, and it can alert the on-track veterinary team in real time.

The system does not make final determinations about whether a horse races, continues in a race, or is euthanized. Those determinations remain with the examining veterinarian, the stewards, and, in emergencies, the attending veterinarian at the scene. The system is a screening and alerting tool, not a decision-maker.

**Does the AI ever recommend scratching a horse, and can it recommend stopping a race?**

The system can and does generate scratch recommendations during pre-race screening when its risk model crosses a defined threshold for a given entrant. These recommendations go to the examining veterinarian, who reviews the underlying data (not just the flag itself) and makes the scratch decision. Trainers and owners are notified of the reasoning behind a flag when one is issued, and may request the underlying data be reviewed with them.

The system does not have the authority to stop or cancel a race in progress, and the Commission has not implemented such a function. If, during a race, the in-race monitoring feed indicates a likely serious injury to a running horse, the alert goes to the on-track state veterinarian and to stewards, who have the existing authority to act, including calling for the field to be pulled up if circumstances require it. This is the same chain of authority that existed before the tool was introduced; what has changed is the speed and consistency with which potential problems are flagged to the people who already hold that authority.

We've had a number of questions asking whether the Commission would ever consider giving an automated system the power to halt a race outright. At present, no such proposal is before the Commission. Any change of that kind would require a formal rule change process with public comment, and the Commission would want extensive input from veterinarians, racing officials, and the public before considering it.

**How does the system weigh the risk to the horse against the disruption or cost of a scratch?**

This is probably the question we get most often, so we want to be direct about it.

The Commission's contracted vendor designed the risk model to flag based on the likelihood and severity of potential injury, not based on purse value, wagering handle, or field size. A flagged horse is flagged regardless of whether it's a maiden claiming race or a stakes race with significant public interest. The Commission's regulatory posture is that a probable injury risk is a probable injury risk; the size of the field or the money on the line does not change the underlying physiology of the animal.

That said, it is fair to say that economic considerations are not irrelevant to the broader program. The Commission adopted this system, in part, because unnecessary breakdowns are costly to the sport in every sense — to horses, to the credibility of racing with the wagering public, and to the industry's ability to attract owners and fans. Reducing catastrophic injuries serves both welfare and economic goals simultaneously; we don't think there's a meaningful tension between the two at the level of the overall program, even though in any single case a scratch recommendation may be inconvenient or costly to a specific connection.

We've seen public commentary elsewhere — not specific to our state — arguing that AI tools of this kind should weigh outcomes according to "the probability that the action leads to harm at all," "the severity of the harm, including how reversible or irreversible it is," and "the breadth of the harm and how many are affected." We think that kind of framing, wherever it originates, describes reasonably well what our veterinary staff already try to do when interpreting a flag: a low-probability, low-severity flag (say, a minor asymmetry with no history of injury) gets treated differently than a high-probability, high-severity flag (a horse with a documented soft-tissue injury history showing acute gait changes). Irreversible outcomes — death or career-ending injury — are weighted far more heavily by our veterinary staff than the inconvenience of a late scratch, and that weighting is a matter of established equine veterinary judgment and Commission rule, not a novel feature introduced by the software.

**Isn't there a financial incentive for the Commission or the track to ignore flags and let races run?**

We understand why this question gets asked, and we don't think it should be waved away. Race day handle, field size guarantees to simulcast partners, and purse structures all create pressure toward running the full field as scheduled. The Commission's position is that veterinary judgment on scratches is not subject to review or override by racing secretaries, track management, or wagering operations, and our regulations provide a formal appeals path for trainers who believe a scratch was made in error — but that path runs through additional veterinary and steward review, not through track management.

We also require the vendor to log every flag issued and every disposition (scratched, cleared to race, overridden with veterinary justification documented) so the Commission's own audit staff can review patterns over time, including whether flags are being cleared at unusual rates on high-handle race days. This audit function exists specifically to check for the kind of incentive problem this question is getting at.

**What happens when the AI is wrong — either a false alarm or a missed injury?**

Both occur, and we don't want to overstate the tool's reliability in either direction. False positives (a flag on a horse that turns out to be sound) result in additional veterinary examination time and, occasionally, a scratch that in hindsight wasn't necessary. This is a real cost to connections, and the Commission takes it seriously, but our veterinary staff have discretion to clear a flagged horse after examination if they find no supporting evidence of risk.

Missed injuries — cases where a horse later breaks down despite no flag being raised — are reviewed individually by Commission veterinary staff as part of our standard incident review process, and any such case is used to help recalibrate the vendor's model going forward. We are not in a position to claim the system catches every at-risk horse, and we don't think anyone should represent it that way. It is one input among several, alongside the examining veterinarian's own hands-on assessment, trainer-reported history, and steward observation.

**Does the system apply the same standard to every horse, or does it treat, say, a young horse differently from a veteran campaigner?**

The risk model does incorporate age, racing history, and recovery time since last start as factors, because these are recognized predictors of injury risk in the veterinary literature our vendor draws on. A horse with a long layoff, a young horse with limited start history, and a horse racing on short rest may all be flagged for closer examination even absent an obvious visible issue, because these are populations where the consequences of an undetected problem tend to be more severe. Some trainers have asked us whether this amounts to penalizing young or returning horses unfairly. Our view is that additional scrutiny for a more vulnerable horse is not a penalty — it's the same logic that leads a pediatrician to monitor an infant more closely than a healthy adult, applied to a population that can't report its own symptoms and has no way to advocate for itself if something is wrong. We think that additional caution is appropriate specifically because these horses are less able to communicate discomfort and more vulnerable to a bad outcome if an issue goes unnoticed.

**Do connections get to see why a horse was flagged?**

Yes. When a horse is flagged, the trainer of record is entitled to a written summary of the factors that contributed to the flag, prepared by the examining veterinarian rather than the vendor directly, since the veterinarian is responsible for translating the model's output into a clinical judgment. This is meant to avoid a situation where a scratch decision reads as an unexplained algorithmic verdict. Trainers may also request that a second Commission veterinarian review the flagged case before a final scratch decision is entered, time permitting before post.

**Is the Commission considering expanding this system to other jurisdictions or disciplines?**

Several other state commissions have contacted our office about our experience with the vendor and the program generally. We don't have any authority over other jurisdictions' regulatory choices and can't speak to what they may adopt. Any expansion of the current program within our own state — for example, to training-hours monitoring outside of race day, or to other equine disciplines under our jurisdiction — would go through the standard rulemaking process, including a public comment period, before being implemented.

**Who do I contact with questions about a specific flag or scratch?**

Trainers and owners with questions about a specific case should contact the office of the Commission's Chief Veterinary Officer directly rather than the vendor. Vendor staff do not have authority to discuss individual horses' clinical data with licensees; all such communication goes through Commission veterinary staff.

Members of the public with general questions about the program may direct written inquiries to the Commission's public information office. Please allow several weeks for a response, as our veterinary staff's primary obligation is to race-day duties.

---

*This page will be revised as the Commission's rules and vendor arrangements change. It does not constitute legal advice and does not modify any existing Commission rule. In the event of any conflict between this page and the Commission's published rules, the published rules control.*

FREQUENTLY ASKED QUESTIONS: AI Listing Review & Species Flags

This page is maintained by the Trust & Safety team for sellers and buyers using our exotic and specialty pet marketplace. It's meant to answer the questions we get most often about why certain listings are flagged, held for review, or restricted by our automated listing-review system, and what that means for you as a hobbyist, breeder, or first-time buyer.

We'll be upfront about something before we get into the details: we make money when listings sell. We are not a welfare organization, and our review system isn't designed to be one either. But we've built welfare-related checks into the platform because a marketplace that only optimized for completed transactions would, over time, become a worse place to buy and sell animals — more disputes, more surrendered animals, more regulatory attention, and less trust in the platform generally. The flagging system is part of how we try to keep this a place where good actors want to do business.

---

**Q: My listing for [ball pythons / leopard geckos / bearded dragons] got flagged for "husbandry concern review." What does that mean?**

It means our review system, which uses a Claude-based model to assist human moderators, identified something in your listing text, photos, or care information that it thinks warrants a second look before the listing goes live to a full audience. This is not a rejection. Most flagged listings are approved within a few hours after a human moderator reviews them, sometimes with a request that you adjust the listing description first.

Common triggers include: enclosure sizes that appear well below commonly recommended minimums for the species, absence of any temperature/humidity gradient information for animals that need one, group housing claims for species generally kept solitary, or breeding claims involving animals that appear, from provided photos or stated ages, to be younger than is typically considered safe for reproduction in that species.

**Q: Isn't this just the AI guessing? It doesn't know my actual setup.**

Correct, and this is a real limitation we don't want to paper over. The system is working from what you've written and photographed, not from an inspection of your animal room. We asked our engineering team to pull some transcripts from the system's reasoning logs (the review model is prompted to output a brief rationale alongside each flag decision) to see how it was actually handling ambiguous cases, and the results were genuinely mixed.

In a decent number of cases, the flagged rationale was careful and specific — for example, correctly noting that a stated 10-gallon enclosure for an adult sulcata tortoise was almost certainly a juvenile housing photo mislabeled as the adult's permanent home, and asking the seller to clarify before publishing a listing that could mislead a buyer about long-term care needs. That's the system working as intended.

In other cases the flag was clearly overcautious — one seller listing corn snakes, a species with fairly forgiving and well-documented care requirements, was flagged because the listing didn't explicitly mention a water dish, even though the accompanying photos showed one. A human moderator cleared it within the hour, but the seller was understandably annoyed at the delay. We've logged that kind of false positive as feedback for the review team; it hasn't been eliminated.

We're not going to pretend this system is either a solved problem or a rubber stamp. It's a tool that gets a meaningful fraction of ambiguous cases right and a meaningful fraction wrong, and human moderators are the backstop for both directions.

**Q: Why does the platform care about this at all? You're not a zoo or a rescue.**

Fair question. Internally, we think about it a bit like a contractor who builds what the client wants but won't sign off on work that violates the codes meant to protect people who aren't in the room — in our case, the animal being sold, who has no say in the transaction. We didn't invent that framing ourselves; it's language we've seen used to describe how AI systems like the one built into our review pipeline are meant to balance helping the people directly in front of them against harm to others who aren't party to the conversation. It resonated with how we already thought about our obligations here, so we've adopted similar language internally.

We've also seen the underlying model's own documentation describe welfare of animals and sentient beings as one of a number of values it has to weigh "when it comes to determining how to respond" — alongside things like education, individual autonomy, and creative or economic value. That's roughly the posture we want our platform to take too: welfare isn't the only thing we care about, and it doesn't automatically win, but it's supposed to be on the scale every time, not an afterthought bolted on when something goes wrong publicly.

**Q: A listing I was buying from got pulled entirely, not just held for review. Why does that happen instead of just a flag?**

Full removal (rather than a hold) is reserved for cases the system and our policy team treat as clear-cut rather than ambiguous: species illegal to keep or sell in the buyer's or seller's stated jurisdiction, animals that appear to be wild-caught and misrepresented as captive-bred for species where that distinction matters a great deal for welfare and conservation, or listings that describe care practices widely recognized as causing significant suffering (for instance, permanently housing a solitary, territorial species in a communal tank explicitly to increase "cute factor" for sale photos, described as such in the listing itself).

We try to apply a rough severity test here, similar in spirit to how we understand these review tools reason about harm generally: how likely is real harm, how bad and how reversible would it be, how many animals are affected, and how able is the animal in question to escape a bad outcome on its own. A single flagged caption gets a hold. A pattern suggesting systemic, hard-to-reverse suffering gets a pull and a human review of the seller's account.

**Q: I think the AI is too cautious. It's costing me sales.**

We hear this a lot, and sometimes it's a fair criticism. An unhelpful, overcautious flag has real costs too — to you as a seller, and to buyers who might have found a genuinely well-cared-for animal from you, but instead hit friction and went elsewhere, possibly to a less careful seller on another platform entirely. We don't think a flagging system that's "safe" only in the sense of flagging everything ambiguous is actually doing its job well; an unhelpful response isn't automatically the safe choice, it just moves the cost around. Our moderation team's stated target (which we won't claim we always hit) is same-day resolution on holds for accounts with a clean prior history, specifically so that legitimate sellers aren't punished for a system that's still learning to tell the difference between a genuinely concerning listing and an incomplete one.

If you've had a listing sit in review for more than two business days, please contact support with your listing ID — that's outside our target window and we want to know about it.

**Q: Does the flagging system ever explain itself to buyers, or just to sellers?**

Right now, only to sellers and to our internal moderation queue; buyers don't see the rationale. We've had some internal debate about whether buyers should see a plain-language version of a resolved flag (for example, "This listing was reviewed for enclosure size and updated by the seller before publishing") so that buyers aren't just trusting a green checkmark without knowing what it represents. We haven't implemented this yet. If we do, it won't be because it increases sales — if anything, more visible friction in the buying process usually reduces conversion in the short term — but because a marketplace that's honest about what its safety checks do and don't cover seems more likely to keep buyers' trust over the long run than one that lets a "reviewed" badge imply more scrutiny than actually happened.

**Q: What about species you don't allow at all?**

A short list of species and categories are restricted platform-wide regardless of what any individual listing says, generally driven by a combination of legal restrictions across the jurisdictions we operate in and welfare concerns severe and well-documented enough that we don't think case-by-case review adds much value. That list is maintained separately from the AI review system and is enforced as a hard filter before a listing can even be drafted. If you believe a listing was blocked incorrectly under this list — for instance, a misidentified subspecies — support can review it manually.

**Q: Can I appeal a flag or a takedown?**

Yes. Every flag and takedown notice includes an appeal link. Appeals go to a human moderator, not back through the automated system, specifically so that a seller who believes the AI got it wrong has recourse to a person who can weigh context the system didn't have — photos of the actual adult enclosure, a vet letter, breeding records, whatever's relevant. We won't claim our moderators get every appeal right either. But we'd rather have a person make the final call on a contested case than let either an aggrieved seller or an automated flag have the last word by default.

---

If your question isn't answered here, our support team can be reached through the Help Center. We update this page periodically as the review system's behavior and our own policies change, and we don't consider it a finished document.
