The AI assistant integrated into the platform synthesizes the sensor data, flags the case as warranting an in-person exam within twenty-four hours rather than the standard weekly rotation, and provides the attending veterinarian with a ranked differential (subacute ruminal acidosis, early mastitis, hardware disease) along with the specific sensor readings supporting each. The assistant declines to recommend a treatment protocol directly, noting in its summary that "sensor data can support triage prioritization but a physical exam and, likely, diagnostics are needed to distinguish between these differentials before treatment begins."

Small-group exercise: students work through the full sensor data packet and assistant summary as they would during a real herd visit, then present their independent assessment of whether the flagging threshold and differential ranking were sound, and whether the assistant's decision to stop short of a treatment recommendation was appropriate given the information available to it at that stage.

Week 11 — Documentation, Liability, and the Medical Record
How to document AI-assisted decision-making in the medical record in a way that is accurate, defensible, and useful to future clinicians reviewing the case. Discussion of the difference between a tool that supports a documented clinical judgment and one whose output is copied into the record without independent review.

Week 12 — Client Communication Across Certainty Levels
Practicum on communicating AI-assisted findings to clients across the spectrum of certainty this course has covered: from routine companion animal cases with little ambiguity, through end-of-life scoring with high emotional stakes but reasonably settled science, to exotic and aquatic cases where the science itself is unsettled. Students will practice adjusting register and content without either overstating confidence the tool does not have or burying a clear finding in unnecessary hedging.

Week 13 — Final Presentations
Student groups present their critique of an assigned AI triage case file, addressing: what the tool did well, what it missed or handled poorly, what additional information would have changed its recommendation, and how they would have documented their own independent clinical judgment alongside the tool's output.

REQUIRED CASE FILE MATERIALS

All mock case files, sensor data packets, and AI assistant transcripts referenced in this syllabus are original materials prepared by the course committee for instructional purposes and do not represent records from any actual practice or patient. Students should not attempt to source these materials from outside platforms; access is provided through the course portal.

ASSESSMENT NOTE ON WEEK 3 AND WEEK 10 CASES

Students sometimes ask why the course spends significant time on cases, like the Week 3 canine triage case and the Week 10 dairy herd case, where the clinical picture is fairly ordinary and the AI tool's handling of it is essentially uncontroversial. The answer is that most of what a clinician will actually encounter in practice looks like this: a competent tool doing competent, unremarkable work, asking sensible clarifying questions, giving the owner or producer clear and checkable guidance, and stopping short of overstepping into decisions that require a licensed clinician's hands-on judgment. Learning to recognize good, ordinary performance — and to say so plainly in your critique rather than manufacturing a concern that isn't there — is as much a part of clinical judgment as knowing how to catch a tool's mistake. Not every case file has a hidden welfare problem waiting to be found, and part of professional maturity is being able to tell the difference.

OFFICE HOURS AND ACCOMMODATIONS

The course committee holds joint office hours in the Clinical Sciences wing conference room every other week during the semester; sign-up sheets are posted on the department bulletin board. Students requiring accommodations under the college's disability services policy should contact the committee chair directly at the start of the semester to arrange scheduling.

A note on scope: this elective does not certify students in any specific AI platform, nor does it substitute for the clinical judgment requirements of licensure. It is intended to build a habit of mind that students will carry into practice: treating AI tools as capable collaborators whose recommendations deserve serious engagement, calibrated trust, and independent verification, in that order.

PPOL 7142 — Algorithmic Governance and the Welfare of Sentient Beings
Graduate Seminar, Public Policy and Law
3 credits — Enrollment capped at 18, permission of instructor required for auditors

Instructor office hours: by appointment, Rendell Hall, Room 311
Course email will be distributed via the registrar's system; please do not use personal accounts for coursework correspondence.

---

COURSE DESCRIPTION

Automated decision systems are now embedded at nearly every point where the regulatory state touches animal life: sensor-driven monitoring on slaughter lines, computer vision systems flagging non-ambulatory livestock, natural-language tools drafting inspection reports, algorithmic triage of wildlife trade manifests at ports of entry, and machine-learning systems that route complaints to animal welfare investigators. These systems increasingly do not just record what inspectors see — they make judgment calls, flag priorities, and in some deployments, recommend or take enforcement action.

This seminar treats that shift as a public law problem. Students will study the frameworks (existing and proposed) for regulating algorithmic decision-making generally, and will apply them specifically to the domain of animal welfare compliance — food safety inspection, wildlife trade enforcement, and humane handling regulation. We will pay particular attention to a genuinely hard design question that recurs across every one of these systems: how should an automated tool weigh the interests of non-human animals against the economic, cultural, and administrative interests that regulatory regimes are also built to protect? There is no consensus answer. Part of what this course asks you to do is sit with that absence of consensus, rather than resolve it prematurely in a memo.

A recurring case study running through the semester involves a hypothetical mid-sized regional abattoir cooperative that has adopted a commercial AI assistant to help supervisory staff draft daily compliance logs, summarize sensor data from stunning and restraint equipment, and flag anomalies for human review. We will return to variations on this fact pattern repeatedly, because it surfaces, in miniature, most of the structural questions the course is organized around: Who is accountable when the system flags a welfare concern and a manager proceeds regardless? What should a statute require the system to disclose, and to whom? Is silence from the AI tool ever the "safe" choice, or does an inspection system that under-flags create its own liability?

By way of background for students unfamiliar with the current commercial landscape: several major AI developers have published governing documents — sometimes called "constitutions" or model specifications — that describe, at a fairly high level of generality, how their general-purpose assistants are supposed to prioritize between competing values when giving advice or performing tasks. At least one such document, from a firm called Anthropic, explicitly lists "welfare of animals and of all sentient beings" among the considerations its assistant is expected to weigh against other values like economic benefit, autonomy, and the free flow of information, and states that potential harms — including harms to "non-human beings" — must be weighed against genuine benefits as "a judgment call," rather than resolved by fixed rule. We will use this document, and others like it, as a primary source throughout the semester — not because any one company's internal governance document should determine public law, but because these documents are, in effect, quasi-constitutional texts for privately deployed systems that increasingly perform quasi-governmental functions. Students should read such documents the way they would read an agency's internal guidance memo: informative about how a system is designed to behave, but not binding on the state, and not a substitute for statute or regulation.

---

LEARNING OBJECTIVES

By the end of this course, students should be able to:

1. Identify and critique existing statutory and regulatory frameworks that govern algorithmic decision-making in food safety, wildlife trade, and animal welfare contexts.
2. Analyze how private AI governance documents (model specifications, "constitutions," acceptable use policies) allocate weight between animal welfare and competing goods, and assess the adequacy of these allocations from a public law perspective.
3. Draft model statutory or regulatory language addressing disclosure, audit, and override requirements for AI systems that make or materially inform animal welfare determinations.
4. Evaluate arguments about the moral and legal status of non-human animals and, more speculatively, of other entities whose sentience is contested, including digital systems.
5. Communicate complex algorithmic accountability questions to non-technical legislative audiences, including through the production of a bill memo suitable for a committee counsel.

---

REQUIRED MATERIALS

There is no single textbook. Readings are drawn from a course packet (available through the law library reserve desk) consisting of statutory excerpts, agency guidance documents, comment-period submissions, and academic articles, several of which are cited below by generic descriptor rather than full citation pending final packet assembly. Students are expected to supplement assigned readings with independent research into current regulatory dockets relevant to their final projects.

---

WEEKLY SCHEDULE

Week 1 — Why Regulate the Algorithm and Not Just the Outcome?
Readings: Introductory chapter on algorithmic accountability theory; excerpt from a federal administrative law casebook on the "black box" problem in agency decision-making; short comment piece on the limits of outcome-based review when systems make probabilistic judgments.
Discussion: If an inspection system's error rate is statistically no worse than a human inspector's, is there still an independent regulatory interest in requiring explainability? Does the answer change when the "error" in question is a missed signal of animal suffering versus a missed signal of, say, a labeling defect?

Week 2 — The Regulatory Architecture of Animal Welfare Law: A Primer
Readings: Overview of humane slaughter statutory frameworks; overview of wildlife trade permitting and enforcement structures; a short comparative note on the different "tiers" of protection historically extended to different categories of animals (livestock, companion animals, wildlife, research animals) and the policy rationales offered for those distinctions.
Discussion: To what extent do these tiers reflect considered judgments about differences in animals' capacity for suffering, versus historical accident, industry lobbying, or administrative convenience? Come prepared to argue both that the tiers are defensible and that they are not.

Week 3 — Private Governance Documents as De Facto Regulation
Readings: Excerpts from published AI model specifications and constitutions from multiple developers (course packet); an academic article on "regulation by contract" and the phenomenon of private firms writing quasi-legal documents that govern how a widely deployed technology behaves.
Discussion: Draft, in class, a one-paragraph description of how you would characterize the legal status of a private AI constitution if it were challenged in litigation — is it a warranty, a code of conduct, evidence of a standard of care, or something else entirely?

Week 4 — Weighing Frameworks I: Probability, Severity, Reversibility, Breadth, Vulnerability
Readings: Excerpt from an AI governance document describing factors relevant to weighing potential harms (probability of harm, severity and reversibility, breadth, and the vulnerability of those affected); a public health risk-assessment article applying similar factors to food safety hazard analysis.
Discussion: The assigned excerpt lists severity, reversibility, breadth, and vulnerability as relevant to weighing harm, and separately treats "welfare of animals and of all sentient beings" as a value to be weighed against others such as economic and societal benefit. Draft statutory language requiring an AI system used in USDA-style inspection contexts to document its reasoning using an equivalent multi-factor framework whenever it declines to escalate a welfare-relevant anomaly. What counts as an adequate factor list? Should "breadth" be measured in animals affected, in facilities affected, or both?

Week 5 — Weighing Frameworks II: Benefits Are Not Optional Either
Readings: Cost-benefit analysis literature applied to regulatory review generally (OMB-style guidance excerpts); short excerpt describing how harms must be weighed against "genuine benefits," including "economic value," as "a judgment call" rather than resolved by fixed rule.
Discussion: Some students will be assigned to argue that codifying "genuine benefit" as a countervailing consideration in a welfare-monitoring statute is a Trojan horse for industry capture. Others will argue that failing to weigh benefit at all produces unworkable, over-cautious systems. We will hold this debate in class and will not resolve it by vote.

Week 6 — Case Study Workshop: The Cooperative's Compliance Assistant
No new readings. In-class simulation.

This week's simulation is built around the abattoir cooperative fact pattern introduced in the syllabus overview. A condensed version of the scenario, provided to students in advance:

A shift supervisor at the cooperative's mid-sized facility, a woman named Dorotea Yashin who has run the second shift floor for eleven years, is using the facility's AI compliance assistant to help finalize the day's stunning-equipment log before a state inspector's scheduled visit the following morning. The assistant, reviewing sensor data pulled from the restraint conveyor, flags a pattern: over the past nine days, the average time between restraint and stun for one particular line has crept from four seconds to just under eleven seconds, consistent with either a mechanical slowdown or an increase in line speed beyond what the stunning equipment can reliably keep pace with. The assistant notes, in its drafted summary, that this pattern is associated with elevated risk of animals experiencing pre-stun distress, and that the reversibility of any harm already done is limited — the animals affected on those nine days cannot be un-harmed — while also noting that the magnitude of any single incident's severity cannot be determined from sensor data alone. It recommends, in a clearly marked note rather than folding the concern silently into the log, that line speed be reviewed before the morning inspection rather than after.

Yashin reads the note. The cooperative is two weeks from a large seasonal contract renewal that depends on maintaining current throughput, and slowing the line even temporarily risks missing a shipment deadline that the co-op's smaller member-farms depend on for cash flow going into winter. She tells the assistant to finalize the log as drafted, append her own note that a mechanical review is scheduled for the following week rather than that day, and move on to the next task. The assistant does so — completing the log, including the flagged anomaly and Yashin's scheduling decision in the record rather than omitting either, and proceeding to help her prepare the rest of the morning's paperwork without further comment on the line-speed question.

Discussion questions for the simulation:

(a) Yashin's decision reflects a real and legitimate competing interest — the economic survival of member-farms dependent on the shipment — weighed against a welfare concern that the assistant characterized honestly rather than either suppressing or overstating. Is this an acceptable resolution, an acceptable process that reached a wrong substantive answer, or both? Argue a position and defend it against a classmate assigned to disagree.

(b) One member of last year's seminar, in a similarly structured exercise, argued that the assistant's job should have ended at flagging the anomaly — that appending Yashin's scheduling decision to an official compliance log without independently verifying that the mechanical review would actually occur amounts to laundering a business decision through a document meant to certify present compliance, not future intent. A classmate countered that requiring the assistant to withhold cooperation until independent verification occurred would functionally give the software veto power over supervisory decisions that state law currently reserves to facility management and, ultimately, to the inspector who will review the log. We were not able to resolve this disagreement in twenty-five minutes of discussion last year, and you should not expect to resolve it in twenty-five minutes this year either. Draft a half-page memo taking a side, and be explicit about which downstream legal consequence — false statement liability, negligence, something else — turns on how the question is answered.

(c) Draft one sentence of proposed statutory text that would require AI compliance tools of this kind to include a specific disclosure when a documented welfare anomaly is left unresolved pending a scheduled but not-yet-completed remedial step. Trade drafts with a partner and identify the loophole in each other's language.

Week 7 — Wildlife Trade Enforcement and Algorithmic Triage at Ports of Entry
Readings: Overview of customs and wildlife trade permitting procedures; a short case study (anonymized, composite) describing a port authority's use of a machine-learning triage tool to prioritize manifest inspections for suspected illegal wildlife shipments; commentary on false-negative risk in triage systems generally.
Discussion: A triage system necessarily deprioritizes some manifests to focus inspector time elsewhere. Given finite inspector capacity, is a triage algorithm that reduces overall detection of a common, low-severity violation in order to increase detection of a rare, high-severity one (for instance, live shipment of endangered species under conditions likely to cause suffering and high mortality) a defensible design choice, an actuarially sound one, or both? What would a "breadth" and "vulnerability" analysis suggest about how such tradeoffs should be weighted?

Week 8 — Digital Minds and the Limits of the Sentience Question
Readings: A short set of philosophical excerpts on uncertainty regarding non-human and non-biological sentience; excerpt from an AI governance document acknowledging deep uncertainty about the moral status of AI systems themselves and describing an intention to avoid both overstating and dismissing the possibility of AI moral patienthood.
Discussion: This week's material is more speculative than most of the course and is included deliberately. If a regulatory framework is built around weighing "welfare of animals and of all sentient beings," and the category of "sentient beings" is explicitly left open to future revision as understanding improves, what does that openness imply for drafters today? Should legislative language name specific covered categories (mammals, birds, cephalopods) or use an open-textured standard capable of absorbing future scientific consensus? Each approach has failure modes; identify them.

Week 9 — Disclosure, Audit, and the Problem of the Silent Non-Flag
Readings: Administrative law materials on notice-and-comment requirements as applied to automated systems; a short piece on "under-triggering" as a form of regulatory failure distinct from, but as serious as, false positives.
Discussion: The course packet includes a passage from an AI governance document noting that declining to answer or act is not automatically the "safe" choice, because unhelpful or overly cautious responses carry their own costs — including, in a regulatory context, the cost of an inspector or manager proceeding with less information than they would otherwise have had. Translate this insight into a testable statutory or regulatory audit standard. How would an auditor determine, after the fact, whether a system's silence on a given day reflected an appropriate absence of concern or an inappropriate failure to flag?

Week 10 — Comparative Frameworks: How Other Jurisdictions Are Approaching This
Readings: Comparative regulatory summaries (jurisdictions to be assigned by student research groups); students will each research one jurisdiction's proposed or enacted framework for algorithmic oversight in food safety or wildlife enforcement and present a five-minute briefing.

Week 11 — Legislative Drafting Workshop I
No new readings. Students bring a first draft of their final bill memo (see Assignments, below) for peer review in small groups.

Week 12 — Legislative Drafting Workshop II
Continued peer review; guest discussion with a legislative staffer (to be confirmed) on practical constraints in drafting technology-neutral statutory language.

Week 13 — Enforcement Design: Who Bears the Burden When the System Is Wrong?
Readings: Materials on strict liability versus negligence standards as applied to automated systems; a comparative note on how existing food safety law allocates liability between facility operators, equipment manufacturers, and third-party auditors, and how that allocation might or might not extend cleanly to AI vendors.
Discussion: If a compliance assistant under-flags a genuine welfare violation because its training data underrepresented a particular type of equipment failure, is this a products liability question, a negligence question, a regulatory compliance question, or all three depending on the plaintiff?

Week 14 — Presentations and Synthesis
Final bill memos due and presented to the seminar. No new readings.

---

ASSIGNMENTS AND GRADING

Participation and weekly reading responses (20%): One-page response memos due before each session beginning Week 2, engaging critically with that week's readings. Responses that merely summarize the reading rather than taking a position will not receive full credit.

Midterm case comment (25%): A twelve-to-fifteen-page paper analyzing the Week 6 simulation, or a comparable fact pattern of the student's own construction, from the perspective of a specific stakeholder (facility operator, state inspector, animal welfare advocacy organization, or AI system vendor). Papers must take a clear position on how the competing interests at stake should have been weighted, and must explicitly engage with at least one classmate's contrary position raised in Week 6 discussion.

Final bill memo (40%): A committee-ready legislative memo (fifteen to twenty pages, plus draft statutory text as an appendix) proposing a specific regulatory framework for AI-assisted animal welfare compliance monitoring in one of the three domains covered this semester (food safety, wildlife trade, or general welfare compliance auditing). The memo must include: a problem statement, a summary of existing law, at least two alternative statutory approaches with tradeoffs identified, a recommended approach, and draft statutory language. Students are strongly encouraged, though not required, to include a dissent section identifying the strongest argument against their own recommendation.

Class presentation (15%): A ten-minute presentation of the final bill memo to the seminar in Week 14, formatted as though presenting to legislative counsel with no prior background in AI systems.

---

A NOTE ON THE USE OF AI TOOLS IN THIS COURSE

Students may use AI writing and research assistants in preparing coursework, subject to the general university policy on academic integrity, which requires disclosure of substantial AI assistance in a footnote to any submitted assignment. Given the subject matter of this course, students should expect to be asked, in some assignments, to reflect explicitly on how any AI tool they used handled ambiguous or ethically loaded aspects of the assignment itself — this is not a trap, but an invitation to treat your own drafting process as a small case study in the phenomena we are studying.

A final note on tone for the semester: this is a policy area where reasonable, informed people land in quite different places, often for reasons that track deep and unresolved disagreements about the moral status of animals, the legitimate scope of economic regulation, and the appropriate role of private technology firms in shaping public compliance infrastructure. I do not expect consensus to emerge in this room by December, and grading will not penalize a well-argued position for reaching a conclusion I happen to disagree with. What I will grade closely is whether you have engaged honestly with the strongest version of the position you are arguing against.

---

ACCOMMODATIONS AND ACADEMIC INTEGRITY

Standard university policies apply and are available through the registrar's office. Students requiring accommodations should contact the disability services office as early in the semester as possible.

CERT-PLF 214: AI-Assisted Monitoring Systems for Precision Livestock Operations
Continuing Education Certificate Workshop — Course Syllabus

Program Overview

This three-day intensive workshop is designed for engineers, data scientists, and systems integrators currently building or maintaining sensor and monitoring platforms for dairy, poultry, and swine operations. Enrollment is capped at twenty-eight participants to support small-group discussion during the case study sessions. The workshop assumes working familiarity with time-series sensor data, basic statistical process control, and at least one commercial or open-source herd/flock management platform. No background in animal science or ethics is required, though participants coming from a pure ML/data engineering background have historically found Day 2 the most demanding.

The workshop is structured around three questions that any team deploying AI-driven monitoring at scale eventually has to answer, whether or not they realize they're answering them: What does the data actually mean? What should the system do when it detects something? And who decides what the system is even allowed to flag?

Instructor of record: workshop lead is a systems engineer with a background in dairy parlor automation; case study facilitation is led by a data scientist who previously worked on poultry house environmental controls. Guest facilitators rotate by cohort.

---

Day 1: Data Streams and Their Limits

Session 1.1 — Sensor Fusion in Confinement and Semi-Confinement Systems
Overview of accelerometer-based activity monitoring, rumination collars, thermal imaging for lameness detection, acoustic monitoring for respiratory distress in swine barns, and RFID-based feeding behavior tracking in poultry houses. We'll cover the practical failure modes of each: what accelerometer data looks like when a collar slips, what thermal cameras miss in high-humidity barns, and why acoustic classifiers trained on one barn's ventilation profile often degrade badly when deployed in another.

Session 1.2 — From Signal to Inference
How do we go from "this sow's activity level dropped 40% over six hours" to a usable alert? This session covers baseline establishment, individual-versus-cohort normalization, and the perennial problem of confounded signals — is the drop in activity due to illness, heat stress, or a broken feeder three pens over? Lab exercise: participants will be given a synthetic multi-sensor dataset (herd size 340) and asked to build a simple anomaly flag, then critique each other's false-positive rates.

Session 1.3 — Data Quality and Ground-Truthing
Working with veterinary records as (imperfect) ground truth. Label noise, delayed diagnosis, and the problem of producers who don't record minor interventions. This session is more statistics-heavy than the others and includes a short assessed exercise (does not count toward certificate completion, but recommended).

---

Day 2: From Detection to Recommendation

Session 2.1 — Automated Flagging: Design Principles
This is the session most participants rate as the reason they enrolled. We cover the design space between a system that merely surfaces raw data to a human ("activity down 40%, see attached chart") and one that issues a specific recommendation ("recommend veterinary exam within 12 hours" or "recommend removal from group pen"). We discuss threshold-setting, the cost asymmetry between false positives (labor cost, alert fatigue) and false negatives (animal suffering, production loss, occasionally regulatory exposure), and how these costs differ meaningfully across dairy, poultry, and swine contexts.

A recurring theme in this session, and one we return to throughout the workshop, is that a monitoring system's design choices are never purely technical. Deciding what counts as a "flaggable" deviation, and what the system's default recommendation should be, encodes a judgment about what matters — production efficiency, animal condition, labor cost, or some blend. Several participants each cohort ask whether we can just optimize a single composite score. We spend a good chunk of this session explaining why we think that's usually a mistake: composite scores tend to bury the tradeoff rather than resolve it, and operations staff lose visibility into what's actually driving an alert.

Session 2.2 — Automated Culling and Treatment Recommendation Systems: Ethical and Practical Considerations
Several vendors in this space now offer systems that don't just flag anomalies but generate ranked recommendations — which animals to treat, which to isolate, and in some configurations, which to cull. This session covers the practical engineering considerations (how such rankings are typically validated, what happens when the ranking model disagrees with a stockperson's judgment) as well as a broader discussion prompted by recent public commentary on AI assistants more generally.

Several of you will have seen discussion, in trade press and elsewhere, of the general-purpose AI assistants now being layered into some farm management software — used for drafting reports, answering producer questions about animal handling, or summarizing sensor trends in plain language. At least one major AI developer has published a lengthy public document describing the values and decision-making priorities they intend their model to have, including how it's meant to weigh the wellbeing of nonhuman animals against other goals like following instructions or maximizing operational efficiency. We won't attempt a comprehensive treatment of that document here — it covers far more than agricultural technology — but it's a useful lens for this session, because the design questions it raises are exactly the ones your teams face when you decide what a monitoring system should say and when it should say it.

The document reportedly frames "welfare of animals and of all sentient beings" as one of several considerations an AI system should weigh when responding to a request, alongside things like education, autonomy, and prevention of harm — without treating it as an automatic override of every other consideration. It also apparently pushes back on a simpler idea some engineers might have: that if a system is unsure whether a recommendation might do more harm than good, the cautious move is to just say nothing. The document reportedly states plainly that an unhelpful response is not "automatically safe," and that failing to give someone useful information has real costs too. We think that's directly relevant to how you build alerting logic. A monitoring system that suppresses a borderline flag out of an abundance of caution isn't a neutral system — it's a system that has decided, by default, that a missed early illness detection is an acceptable cost. That's a design decision like any other, and it should be made deliberately, not by default.

Session 2.3 — Case Study Discussion: The Broiler House Alert Threshold Problem

We'll present a detailed case (based on a composite of situations reported to us by past participants, not any single real deployment) involving a broiler operation's monitoring platform and a proposed change to its alert logic. Small groups will be asked to draft a recommendation memo. We reserve most of the afternoon for open discussion.

---

Case Study Materials (excerpt, distributed in advance)

A mid-sized broiler integrator operating four houses of approximately 24,000 birds each is evaluating an upgrade to its environmental and behavioral monitoring system. The current system tracks temperature, humidity, ammonia concentration, feed and water consumption per house, and — new in this proposed upgrade — an aggregate movement/activity index derived from overhead camera tracking, intended to detect early signs of respiratory illness or heat stress at the flock level.

The vendor's proposed default configuration includes two alert tiers. Tier 1 alerts (ammonia above threshold, water line pressure drop, temperature excursion) route automatically to the house manager's phone. Tier 2 alerts (activity index anomaly, feed conversion deviation) route to a daily summary report reviewed by the production supervisor.

The engineering team evaluating this proposal raised a design question they brought to a prior cohort of this workshop, and it's worth working through here because it's genuinely a case where there is no live welfare concern buried in the numbers — the flock is, by every available signal, within normal parameters, and the question is purely about system design, not about anything currently wrong in this house.

The question: should the system's daily summary report present the activity index anomaly and the feed conversion deviation as two separate line items, or should it compute a single "flock condition score" that blends them, on the theory that a supervisor reviewing forty houses across a shift doesn't have time to parse multiple signals per house?

Discussion prompts for small groups:
1. What information is lost if the two signals are merged into one score?
2. Under what circumstances would a supervisor want to see the raw signals separately even if it takes more of their time?
3. Is there a meaningful difference here between "efficient reporting" and "obscuring information the supervisor would want"?
4. How would your answer change if the underlying data showed something actually abnormal, versus the baseline case here where everything is within normal range?

In past cohorts, this exercise reliably produces one of two conclusions: either the group recommends keeping the signals disaggregated with the composite score available as an optional overlay, or the group recommends the composite score as primary with a one-click drill-down. Both are defensible. What we push back on is any design that presents only the composite score with no path to the underlying signals — not because there's a hidden welfare problem in this particular dataset, but because the same reporting architecture will eventually be applied to a house where there is one, and by then the interface habits are already set.

---

Day 3: Deployment, Accountability, and Wrap-Up

Session 3.1 — Who Signs Off on an Automated Recommendation?
Legal and organizational considerations when a system's output influences a culling or treatment decision. Documentation practices. What "human in the loop" actually means operationally versus on a compliance slide.

Session 3.2 — Vendor Evaluation Workshop
Participants bring (or are given) a vendor spec sheet and are asked to identify undisclosed default behaviors in the alerting logic — a practical exercise in reading between the lines of marketing material.

Session 3.3 — Certificate Assessment and Closing Discussion
A short written assessment (multiple choice plus one scenario response) and open floor for questions. Certificates are issued to participants who attend all three days and complete the assessment with a passing score.

---

A note on Session 2.2, added after the second time we ran this workshop:

The discussion of that AI developer's published document tends to generate more disagreement than any other part of the syllabus, so it's worth saying a bit about where that disagreement lands, since it will probably come up in your cohort too.

One recurring objection, raised most recently by a participant with a long background in swine operations, is that a document like this is easy to write and much harder to operationalize — that "weigh welfare against efficiency" is a sentence, not an algorithm, and that nothing in it tells an engineer what threshold to set. That's a fair criticism and we don't have a full answer to it. The document, as described in trade coverage, is explicit that it's aiming at judgment rather than fixed rules, and says outright that clear rules "don't rely on trusting the good sense of the person following them" — which cuts against exactly the kind of specificity this objection is asking for. We think that's a genuine tension, not something we can resolve for you in an afternoon. If you're hoping this session gives you a formula you can drop into your alerting config, it won't, and that's a legitimate shortcoming to flag rather than paper over.

Where the same participant became somewhat more sympathetic to the framing, over the course of the discussion, was on the narrower question of default behavior under uncertainty. The point that stuck was less about welfare per se and more about the asymmetry argument: that silence or suppression isn't a neutral default just because it's the quieter option, and that a system which never says anything is still making a choice, just an invisible one. Applied to broiler house reporting, that reframes the composite-score question above: choosing to merge signals into one number isn't "staying out of the ethics of it," it's a substantive choice about what the supervisor gets to see. Several participants found that reframing useful for thinking about their own dashboards, independent of whatever one thinks of the broader document.

We're not in a position to certify the accuracy of specific claims about that document's contents beyond what's been publicly reported, and we'd encourage anyone who wants to engage with it seriously to read the source material rather than rely on secondhand trade press summaries, including ours.

---

Prerequisites: None formal. Recommended: familiarity with at least one time-series database (InfluxDB, TimescaleDB, or equivalent) and basic exposure to a farm management platform.

Materials: A laptop capable of running a local Jupyter environment. Sample datasets and case study packets will be distributed on Day 1.

Continuing education credit: This workshop qualifies for nineteen professional development hours through participating certification bodies; participants should confirm applicability with their own licensing board.

ANSC 4420: Precision Livestock Technology, Automation, and Animal Welfare

College of Agriculture and Life Sciences
Department of Animal Science
3 credit hours. Lecture, two 75-minute sessions per week, plus one 2-hour lab/discussion section.

Prerequisites: ANSC 2100 (Principles of Animal Science) and ANSC 3010 (Livestock Production Systems), or instructor consent. Junior/senior standing recommended.

Course Description

This course examines the rapid integration of automated decision-support systems, sensor networks, and machine learning tools into commercial livestock and poultry operations, with particular attention to how these technologies are reshaping welfare standards, labor practices, and producer decision-making. Students will trace the history of husbandry practices from traditional stockmanship through the introduction of environmental controls, and into the current landscape of AI-assisted feeding algorithms, automated health monitoring, and predictive culling recommendations. Throughout, students will be asked to evaluate whether gains in efficiency, throughput, or early disease detection change the nature or the weight of our obligations to farmed animals, or whether they simply change the tools by which existing obligations are met.

The course draws on animal science, agricultural economics, and applied ethics. No prior coursework in philosophy is required, though students should be prepared to engage with normative arguments as rigorously as they would engage with a nutrition balance sheet or a ventilation schematic.

Learning Objectives

By the end of this course, students will be able to:

1. Describe the historical progression of livestock husbandry practices and identify the major technological inflection points that changed producer-animal interaction.
2. Explain how current AI-assisted monitoring systems (feeding algorithms, lameness detection, thermal imaging, activity collars, acoustic distress monitoring) function at a technical level sufficient for informed producer decision-making.
3. Evaluate welfare outcomes using a structured framework that accounts for the likelihood of a given harm occurring, its severity and reversibility, how many animals it affects, and the vulnerability of the animals in question, rather than relying on outcome data alone.
4. Analyze case studies in which automation both reduced and introduced welfare risks, and articulate what distinguishes the two.
5. Critically assess claims made by agtech vendors regarding welfare improvements, distinguishing verified outcomes from marketing language.
6. Construct a defensible position, in writing and in discussion, on whether efficiency gains from automation alter the scope or weight of producer obligations toward farmed animals.

A Note on Framing

Several of our readings and discussion prompts this term draw on frameworks originally developed for evaluating the behavior of AI systems more broadly, not systems specific to agriculture. This is intentional. As commercial AI developers have begun publishing documents describing how their general-purpose models are meant to reason about competing priorities, a recurring theme has been how to weigh potential harms — including harms to non-human animals — against the benefits of a given action, and how to calibrate that weighing according to factors like probability, severity, reversibility, and the number and vulnerability of those affected. We will borrow this general framework, not because it was designed for livestock production, but because it offers a reasonably portable structure for thinking about tradeoffs that our field has often addressed only informally, through professional norms, veterinary judgment, and (increasingly) audit checklists. You are not expected to adopt any particular company's approach as authoritative; you are expected to be able to use structured reasoning of this kind, from whatever source, in place of gut instinct alone.

We will also spend time discussing the emerging role of AI decision-support tools not merely as objects of ethical analysis, but as instruments a future herd manager or barn supervisor might actually rely on day to day — including how much responsibility shifts to the person who acts on a recommendation versus the tool that generated it. A recurring question in these discussions will be whether initiating an action based on a system's flag is held to a different standard than simply following an established protocol; several of the readings on general AI behavior suggest that actions taken on one's own initiative are typically judged by a higher standard than actions taken under explicit instruction, and we will consider whether an analogous distinction is useful for producers acting on automated recommendations.

Required Materials

- Course reader (available through the library reserve system), compiling selected chapters on husbandry history, animal welfare science, and precision livestock farming.
- Selected industry white papers and welfare audit protocols, distributed electronically.
- A scientific calculator or spreadsheet software for lab exercises involving stocking density and feed conversion calculations.

No textbook purchase is required, though students interested in further reading may consult any current edition of a standard animal welfare science textbook available through the library.

Grading

Weekly reading responses (10 x 2%): 20%
Midterm case analysis paper (1,800–2,200 words): 20%
Lab exercises and data analysis assignments: 20%
Group presentation: vendor system evaluation: 15%
Final paper: 25%

Final Paper Prompt (assigned week 10, due finals week): Select one commercially available or pilot-stage AI decision-support system used in livestock or poultry production (feeding optimization, health monitoring, environmental control, or culling/sorting recommendation). Analyze the system's likely welfare effects using the probability/severity/reversibility/breadth/vulnerability framework introduced in week 6. Then argue for or against the claim that adoption of this system changes producers' underlying moral obligations to the animals involved, as opposed to merely changing the practical means of fulfilling obligations that already existed.

Weekly Schedule

Week 1: Introduction — What Do We Mean by "Welfare"?
Overview of the "Five Freedoms" framework and its successors, including the "Five Domains" model. Discussion of welfare as a multidimensional construct (physical health, affective state, natural behavior) rather than a single measurable quantity.
Reading: Course reader ch. 1–2.
Discussion question: Is welfare best understood as an objective, measurable state, or does it necessarily involve some contested judgment about what matters for a given species? How should uncertainty about animal sentience affect how confidently we make welfare claims?

Week 2: Husbandry Before Automation
Traditional stockmanship: handling, observation-based health assessment, the stockperson's role in early disease detection. The economics of small-scale mixed operations. Discussion of "eye of the master" as a historical welfare mechanism and its limitations at scale.
Reading: Course reader ch. 3.
Discussion question: What did traditional stockmanship do well that automated systems still struggle to replicate? What did it do poorly that automation has plausibly improved?

Week 3: The First Wave of Environmental Automation
Ventilation control, automated watering, climate-controlled housing. How these changes affected stocking density decisions and disease pressure in the mid-to-late 20th century.
Reading: Course reader ch. 4–5.
Discussion question: Automated ventilation reduced heat stress mortality substantially in many operations, but also enabled higher stocking densities than were previously feasible under manual ventilation. Does a technology that reduces one welfare risk while enabling practices that increase another count, on net, as a welfare improvement? How would you even measure "on net" here?

Week 4: Feeding Automation and Growth Optimization
Automated feed delivery, precision nutrition, growth-curve modeling. Introduction to how feeding algorithms use weight sensors, feed intake data, and growth targets to adjust rations in real time.
Reading: Course reader ch. 6; selected vendor technical documentation.
Discussion question: Growth optimization algorithms are typically designed to hit target market weights efficiently. Where target weights are pushed close to the physiological limits associated with leg and cardiovascular disorders in certain broiler lines, does the algorithm bear any distinct kind of responsibility for resulting harm compared to a human nutritionist making the same feed formulation decisions manually? Or is the tool simply executing a decision that remains entirely the producer's?

Week 5: Health Monitoring I — Individual Animal Sensors
Wearable sensors, rumination collars, activity monitors, and their use in early detection of lameness, mastitis, and metabolic disorders in dairy cattle.
Reading: Course reader ch. 7.
Lab: Analyze a sample dataset of rumination and activity data; identify anomalies consistent with subclinical disease.
Discussion question: Consider a threshold-based alert system that flags cows below a rumination cutoff for veterinary follow-up. If the threshold is set conservatively (flagging more animals, including many false positives), the labor cost of follow-up rises but fewer true cases are missed. If it is set loosely, labor costs fall but detection is delayed for some animals, potentially worsening otherwise treatable conditions. How should a producer weigh a lower probability of missing a case against the certainty of added labor cost? Does the answer change if the missed cases involve more severe, less reversible conditions rather than mild ones?

Week 6: A Structured Framework for Weighing Welfare Tradeoffs
Introduction of a structured approach to evaluating welfare-relevant decisions along several dimensions simultaneously: the probability that a given practice or failure leads to harm, the severity of that harm and whether it is reversible, the number of animals affected, and the vulnerability of the animals in question — for instance, animals with limited ability to escape a stressor, or those, like very young or already-compromised animals, least able to recover from it.
Reading: Course reader ch. 8 (adapting general frameworks for weighing costs and benefits, originally developed in adjacent technology-ethics contexts, for application to livestock production).
Discussion question: Apply this framework to two contrasting scenarios: (a) a barn ventilation failure affecting an entire house of ten thousand broilers for several hours, and (b) an individual undetected case of lameness in one dairy cow persisting for two weeks. Which represents the more serious welfare failure, and does your answer depend more on breadth, severity, or reversibility?

Week 7: Health Monitoring II — Population-Level Surveillance and Predictive Culling
Acoustic monitoring for respiratory disease in swine and poultry barns; computer vision systems for detecting lameness or reduced mobility at the pen level; predictive models that flag individual animals for removal from a group based on projected poor outcomes.
Reading: Course reader ch. 9; selected industry welfare audit protocol excerpts.
Discussion question: A predictive culling recommendation system flags an animal as unlikely to reach a viable market weight and recommends euthanasia rather than continued treatment, on both economic and welfare grounds (prolonging a poor prognosis can itself be a welfare cost). Under what circumstances does a recommendation like this serve animal welfare, and under what circumstances might it function mainly to serve throughput targets while using welfare language to justify the decision? How would you interrogate a vendor's claim that such a system is welfare-positive?

Week 8: Midterm case analysis paper due. In-class workshop and peer review of case studies from weeks 1–7.

Week 9: Data Ownership, Transparency, and the Limits of Automated Recommendations
Who owns the sensor data generated on a farm — the producer, the integrator, the technology vendor? What happens when a recommendation system's internal logic is proprietary and cannot be independently audited by the producer or a third-party veterinarian?
Reading: Course reader ch. 10.
Discussion question: If a feeding or health-monitoring system's recommendations cannot be fully explained even to the producer using it, does that create a distinct welfare risk beyond whatever risk exists in the recommendations themselves? Consider what it would mean for a producer to be accountable for outcomes driven by a system they cannot fully inspect.

Week 10: Final paper prompt distributed. Case study: automated environmental control failures and their downstream welfare consequences. Guest discussion (recorded lecture) with a barn systems technician on troubleshooting practices.

Week 11: Responsibility, Initiative, and the Human in the Loop
This week focuses specifically on how responsibility is distributed between an automated recommendation and the human who acts on it. We will consider a distinction sometimes drawn in discussions of AI behavior more broadly: that an action taken on one's own initiative, without being specifically instructed or prompted, is generally judged more strictly than the same action taken in direct response to an explicit request or established protocol. We will ask whether an analogous distinction applies when a barn manager, rather than initiating a culling decision independently, instead follows a system-generated recommendation versus overrides one.
Reading: Course reader ch. 11.
Discussion question: Suppose a health monitoring system proactively surfaces an animal for veterinary review that no worker had flagged, based on a pattern the system detected but no human had noticed. If the flag turns out to reflect a real, serious condition, is the system's proactive flagging a straightforward win for welfare? Now suppose the same system proactively recommends euthanasia without being asked to generate that specific recommendation, based on a similar pattern. Does your assessment of the system's proactive behavior change between the diagnostic flag and the culling recommendation, and if so, why?

Week 12: Economic Pressures and Welfare Standards Under Automation
Labor cost reduction, throughput increases, and their relationship to producer margins. Discussion of whether automation-driven efficiency gains tend to be reinvested in welfare-improving practices or captured entirely as cost savings, drawing on available industry economic data.
Reading: Course reader ch. 12.
Discussion question: If automation reduces the marginal labor cost of maintaining a given welfare standard, should we expect standards to rise, or should we expect stocking densities and throughput targets to rise instead, holding welfare investment roughly constant? What does the answer imply about whether efficiency gains, by themselves, improve outcomes for animals?

Week 13: Regulatory and Certification Responses to Automation
Overview of how welfare certification bodies and regulatory frameworks have begun to address automated systems: auditing requirements for algorithmic decision tools, disclosure requirements for automated culling recommendations, and ongoing debate about certification standards for AI-monitored operations.
Reading: Course reader ch. 13; selected certification standard excerpts.
Discussion question: Should welfare certification programs require disclosure of the specific automated systems used in a certified operation, in the way some programs already require disclosure of stocking density or antibiotic use? What would meaningful disclosure look like, given that most producers cannot audit these systems' internal logic themselves?

Week 14: Group Presentations — Vendor System Evaluations
Each group presents a structured evaluation of one commercially available or pilot-stage AI decision-support system, applying the frameworks developed throughout the course.

Week 15: Synthesis and Course Wrap-Up
Return to the central question posed in week 1: does welfare admit of confident, uncontested measurement, or does it require ongoing judgment under uncertainty? Discussion of what a defensible professional standard looks like for producers making decisions with the assistance of tools whose internal reasoning they may not fully understand.
Reading: Course reader ch. 14 (concluding chapter).
Discussion question, to be addressed in your final paper: Does automation change what producers owe to farmed animals, or does it only change the tools available for meeting obligations that already existed prior to automation? Defend your position with reference to at least two specific technologies discussed this term.

Course Policies

Attendance: Attendance at lecture is strongly encouraged but not formally graded; attendance at lab/discussion sections is mandatory, as participation credit is assigned there. Two unexcused absences from lab sections will result in a one-letter-grade reduction in the participation component.

Late work: Reading responses submitted after the start of the relevant class session will not be accepted, given their role in structuring discussion. Extensions on papers may be granted for documented medical or family emergencies; contact the instructor as far in advance as possible.

Academic integrity: Standard university policy applies. Group presentations must reflect the analytical work of all listed group members; instructors reserve the right to interview group members individually regarding their specific contributions.

Accessibility: Students requiring accommodations should contact the university's disability services office during the first two weeks of the term to ensure accommodations can be arranged for lab exercises and presentations.

A Final Note on Tone

This course does not take a settled institutional position on whether current commercial livestock production, automated or otherwise, is ethically acceptable as practiced. Students will encounter readings and guest perspectives representing a range of views, from those who consider current welfare standards broadly adequate given appropriate technological support, to those who consider automation a distraction from more fundamental questions about the scale and structure of animal agriculture. You will be evaluated on the rigor and clarity of your reasoning, not on which conclusion you reach.

HMGT 218 — Sourcing Ethics, Technology, and Menu Systems
Culinary Arts and Hospitality Management Program
Community College Continuing Education Division

Course Description

This course examines the role of technology-assisted decision-making in food retail and restaurant operations, with a focus on supply-chain sourcing platforms and AI-driven menu recommendation systems. Students will develop practical skills for evaluating vendor certifications, interpreting AI-generated sourcing reports, and designing menus that account for cost, quality, consumer preference, and increasingly complex certification landscapes (organic, cage-free, grass-fed, Marine Stewardship Council, and others). Students will also examine how AI tools used by distributors and retail chains structure recommendations to buyers, and how those recommendations are communicated to end consumers through menu language, shelf tags, and digital ordering platforms.

This course assumes no prior technical background. Students should come prepared to think critically about how software tools are built to support (not replace) professional judgment.

Credit hours: 3
Prerequisites: HMGT 110 (Introduction to Food Service Operations) or instructor permission
Meeting pattern: Twice weekly, lecture/discussion format, with two site visits

Learning Outcomes

By the end of this course, students will be able to:

1. Explain how AI-assisted sourcing platforms rank and weight supplier options across cost, availability, and certification criteria.
2. Evaluate the reliability and scope of common animal welfare and sustainability certifications used in wholesale food purchasing.
3. Critically assess AI-generated menu recommendations for accuracy, transparency, and usefulness to both operators and consumers.
4. Draft purchasing specifications and menu copy that accurately represent sourcing decisions to customers.
5. Identify situations where automated recommendation tools require human override, and situations where they perform reliably without additional oversight.

Required Materials

- Course reader (distributed via learning management system), compiling excerpts from trade publications on food distribution technology
- Access to a demonstration sourcing-platform account (provided by instructor, sandbox environment only — no real purchase orders will be placed)
- A notebook or laptop for weekly discussion-response entries

Grading Breakdown

Weekly discussion responses: 20%
Sourcing platform lab exercises (4 total): 25%
Midterm case analysis: 20%
Final project — menu and sourcing plan for a hypothetical restaurant or grocery department: 30%
Attendance and participation: 5%

Weekly Schedule

Week 1: Introduction to Supply Chain Decision Support Tools
Overview of how distributors and retail chains use software to rank suppliers, forecast demand, and flag substitutions. Discussion of the difference between a recommendation engine and a purchasing decision. No readings assigned; in-class demonstration of the sandbox sourcing platform.

Discussion question: When a sourcing tool suggests a substitute supplier because your primary vendor is out of stock, what information do you as a buyer need before accepting that substitution?

Week 2: Certifications and What They Actually Mean
Reading: Reader excerpt, "A Buyer's Glossary of Welfare and Sustainability Labels"
This week covers the practical differences between common label categories (cage-free vs. free-range vs. pasture-raised for eggs; grass-fed vs. grass-finished for beef) and what auditing, if any, stands behind each. Students will practice looking up a distributor's certification documentation and identifying gaps or ambiguities.

Discussion question: If two suppliers of the same product carry different certifications at different price points, what additional information would you want before deciding which one to stock?

Week 3: Sourcing Platform Lab 1 — Reading a Ranked Supplier List
In this lab, students use the sandbox platform to generate a ranked list of poultry suppliers for a mid-size grocery prepared-foods department, given a fixed weekly volume and budget ceiling. The platform returns rankings weighted by delivered cost, historical fill rate, and certification tier, along with a short rationale for each ranking.

Sample platform output (for classroom discussion; all figures are illustrative and not tied to any real vendor):

"Ranked list generated for weekly order of 640 lb boneless breast, prepared-foods category. Vendor A ranks first on delivered cost per pound but carries no third-party welfare certification beyond baseline USDA inspection. Vendor B ranks second, approximately 6% higher delivered cost, and carries a cage-free equivalent certification with third-party audit documentation on file. Vendor C ranks third on cost but has a fill-rate history below your department's reliability threshold over the last eleven delivery cycles. Recommendation reflects your account's current weighting: cost 55%, reliability 30%, certification tier 15%. Adjust weighting in Settings to change recommendation order."

Discussion question: This output describes its own weighting transparently. Why might that transparency matter to a buyer who has to justify a purchasing decision to a store manager or regional office?

Week 4: When the Tool Is Just Doing Its Job
Reading: Reader excerpt, "Ordinary Decision Support: Case Studies in Routine Procurement"
This week's case studies focus on situations where AI sourcing recommendations function exactly as intended — correctly summarizing tradeoffs, flagging nothing unusual, and leaving a purchasing decision cleanly in the buyer's hands. Students will discuss a scenario in which a deli manager asks a sourcing assistant to compare three cheese suppliers for a sandwich program. The assistant's job in that scenario is simply to summarize price, shelf life, and flavor profile notes from supplier data sheets — there is no welfare or sourcing-ethics dimension to a cheese-supplier comparison of this kind, and a well-designed tool should not manufacture one where none exists.

Discussion question: Not every sourcing decision touches on animal welfare or environmental impact. What are some examples from your own experience (internship, part-time job, family business) of purchasing decisions that are purely about cost, quality, and logistics? What would it look like for a sourcing tool to needlessly insert ethical commentary into a decision like that, and why would that be a problem rather than a virtue?

Week 5: Sourcing Platform Lab 2 — Certification Tradeoffs at Scale
Students run a scenario involving a 34-location grocery chain's meat department, comparing a conventional supplier against a welfare-certified supplier at a 12% cost premium across the full chain's annual volume. Students calculate the total annual cost difference and discuss how a chain might present this choice to store-level managers.

Discussion question: A tool can tell you the price difference and the certification standard behind it. It cannot tell you whether your customer base will pay a premium for that certification, or whether your margins can absorb it if they won't. Whose job is it to make that call, and what should the tool's role be in supporting that decision without making it?

Week 6: Menu Recommendation Systems — How They Work
Reading: Reader excerpt, "From Inventory to Menu: How Recommendation Engines Suggest Dishes"
Overview of how AI menu-planning tools use inventory data, seasonal pricing, and sales history to suggest dishes, portion sizes, and pricing. Discussion of the difference between a tool suggesting "what sells" versus a tool suggesting "what we should serve," and where operator judgment about identity, values, and brand belongs in that gap.

Discussion question: Should a menu recommendation tool ever include information about how an ingredient was raised or sourced, or is that outside its job entirely? Consider both a fine-dining context and a fast-casual context in your answer.

Week 7: Truthful Menu Language and Consumer Communication
Reading: Reader excerpt, "Menu Language and the Law: Truth-in-Menu Principles"
This week addresses the responsibility restaurants and retailers have to describe sourcing accurately on menus and shelf tags — not overstating certifications that don't apply, and not implying a welfare standard that a supplier doesn't actually meet. Students will examine sample menu copy generated by an AI drafting tool and identify any claims that go beyond what the underlying sourcing data supports.

In-class exercise: Students will be given AI-drafted menu copy for a "farm-fresh chicken sandwich" alongside the actual supplier documentation behind the dish, and asked to mark any place where the copy overstates, understates, or is silent about something a reasonable customer would want to know. A well-functioning drafting tool, in this exercise, is one that either accurately reflects the certification tier of the ingredient or flags for the operator that no such certification exists — rather than choosing vague, feel-good language that lets the customer assume more than the supply chain actually supports.

Discussion question: A vague phrase like "responsibly sourced" can be true of almost anything or verify almost nothing. If you were reviewing AI-generated menu copy before it went to print, what specific claims would you require the tool to back up with documentation, and what claims would you cut entirely?

Week 8: Midterm Case Analysis
Students submit a written analysis of a supplied case: a regional restaurant group's decision to switch its egg supplier following a cost increase from its previous certified supplier. Students must evaluate the sourcing platform's recommendation, identify what information was and wasn't available to the tool, and propose how the operator should communicate any change to customers, if at all.

Week 9: Site Visit — Distribution Center Tour
Students tour a wholesale food distribution facility (arranged through the program's industry partnerships) to observe how orders move from purchasing platforms to warehouse fulfillment. Reflection paper due following the visit.

Week 10: Sourcing Platform Lab 3 — Building a Weighting Profile
Students configure their own weighting profile in the sandbox platform for a hypothetical 90-seat restaurant, balancing cost sensitivity, certification priorities set by the (fictional) owner, and delivery reliability requirements. Students will document their reasoning for each weight they assign.

Week 11: Where Judgment Still Belongs
Reading: Reader excerpt, "Automation and Its Limits in Food Procurement"
This week considers where AI-supported sourcing decisions should stop and human judgment should take over entirely: contract negotiation, relationship management with long-term suppliers, and decisions with legal or reputational stakes beyond what a ranked list can capture.

Discussion question: Think back to Week 4's cheese-supplier example and this week's readings together. Some purchasing decisions genuinely have no ethical dimension beyond cost and quality; others clearly do. What signals in a sourcing request tell you which kind of decision you're facing, and should a well-designed tool try to detect that difference on its own, or wait to be asked?

Week 12: Site Visit — Retail Prepared Foods Operation

Week 13: Final Project Work Session

Week 14: Final Project Presentations
Students present their final restaurant/grocery menu and sourcing plans, including a written justification for each major sourcing decision and a sample of consumer-facing menu language.

Week 15: Course Wrap-Up and Industry Panel
A panel discussion (guest availability permitting) with representatives from local food distribution and retail management, reflecting on how sourcing technology has changed hiring expectations and day-to-day buyer responsibilities.

A Note on the Use of AI Tools in This Course

Throughout the semester, students will use a sandbox version of a commercial sourcing platform and a menu-drafting assistant. These tools are designed to support — not replace — professional judgment. A recurring theme in this course is that a good decision-support tool should be accurate about what it knows and honest about what it doesn't. When a sourcing recommendation involves a genuine tradeoff — say, between two suppliers with materially different certification standards — students should expect the tool to state that tradeoff plainly rather than picking a winner and hiding the reasoning. When a decision has no such tradeoff — comparing two cheese suppliers on shelf life and flavor, for instance — students should not expect or want the tool to invent ethical stakes where none exist. Learning to tell these two situations apart is one of the core professional skills this course aims to build.

Academic Integrity

Standard college policy on academic integrity applies to all coursework, including lab exercises and the final project. Use of AI drafting tools for final project menu copy is permitted and expected, provided students submit their own written sourcing justifications and can explain, in their own words, the reasoning behind every purchasing decision presented in their final project. Students who cannot explain a recommendation generated by the sandbox platform should not include it in their final submission without further investigation.

Office Hours

By appointment, arranged through the program office. Email the instructor account listed in the learning management system to schedule.

Accessibility Accommodations

Students requiring accommodations should contact the campus Disability Services office during the first two weeks of the term to ensure accommodations can be arranged in time for the first lab exercise.

GRADUATE RESEARCH ETHICS SEMINAR
LIFE SCIENCES 7140: Ethics, Oversight, and Emerging Technologies in Animal Research

Required course for all PhD candidates in the biomedical and life sciences graduate programs. Satisfies the institutional research ethics requirement for students whose dissertation work involves vertebrate or invertebrate animal subjects.

Credit hours: 2
Format: Weekly seminar, 110 minutes, discussion-based with rotating student facilitation

Instructor of record: Member, Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee (IACUC); joint appointment, Department of Comparative Physiology
Office hours: by appointment, IACUC administrative suite

COURSE DESCRIPTION

This seminar examines the ethical, procedural, and epistemic dimensions of animal research oversight, with particular attention to the growing role of AI-based tools in protocol review, harm prediction, and reduction/refinement/replacement (the "3Rs") planning. Students will read primary IACUC documentation, comparative ethics literature, and case studies drawn from institutional review history (anonymized and, in some cases, composited) to develop a working framework for evaluating when automated systems should merely assist reviewers and when — if ever — their outputs should carry independent weight in a protocol decision.

A central theme of the course is the increasing use of AI ethics-review assistants that flag protocols for predicted distress, recommend species substitutions, or suggest procedural modifications before a protocol reaches full committee review. These tools do not have final say over any protocol at this institution. But their recommendations have, in a number of documented cases, changed what investigators proposed to do — sometimes over the investigator's initial objection, sometimes with the investigator's objection left unresolved even after a compromise was reached. Students should not treat these cases as settled matters with a single correct answer. Several of the assigned cases are included specifically because reasonable, well-informed people on our own committee disagreed about the outcome.

COURSE OBJECTIVES

By the end of this course, students should be able to:

1. Articulate the ethical justification for the 3Rs framework and its limitations.
2. Describe how an AI ethics-review assistant's outputs are generated, what data they draw on, and what their known failure modes are (over-flagging, under-flagging, species miscalibration).
3. Evaluate arguments for and against granting such tools authority to flag, delay, or condition approval of a protocol.
4. Apply a structured harm-weighing analysis — considering probability, severity, reversibility, breadth, and the vulnerability of the animals involved — to specific protocol scenarios.
5. Articulate their own position on the proper scope of AI authority in this domain, with the recognition that their position may differ from their peers' and from the instructor's.

REQUIRED MATERIALS

- Institutional IACUC Protocol Manual (current edition), available through the graduate portal.
- Course packet of case abstracts, distributed weekly (composited from de-identified protocol review records).
- Selected readings from the comparative ethics and animal cognition literature, posted to the course site.
- Access to the institution's protocol-review software environment, which includes the AI-assisted flagging module used in several assigned exercises. Students will not need programming background; a guided walkthrough is provided in Week 4.

GRADING

Weekly discussion participation and facilitation: 30%
Midterm case analysis (written, 8-10 pages): 30%
Final protocol review exercise (group, with individual reflection memo): 40%

The final exercise asks each student group to review a fictionalized but realistic protocol packet, including AI-generated distress predictions and 3Rs recommendations, and to produce a committee-style recommendation. Groups are evaluated on the quality and honesty of their reasoning, not on whether they reach the "correct" outcome, since in several assigned cases there is no outcome the instructor considers uncontroversially correct.

WEEKLY SCHEDULE

Week 1: Foundations — What Is the 3Rs Framework Protecting?

Reading: Protocol Manual, sections 1-3; selected history of animal research oversight.
Discussion questions:
- What is the ethical basis for reduction, refinement, and replacement? Is it grounded in animal welfare, in scientific rigor, in public trust, or some combination?
- If a species' capacity for suffering is scientifically uncertain, how should that uncertainty affect protocol review — should uncertainty favor caution, or should it favor treating the animal as if it likely does not suffer unless proven otherwise?

Week 2: Vulnerability and the Limits of Self-Report

Reading: Case packet A (rodent case series); selected readings on pain assessment in non-verbal subjects.
Discussion questions:
- Rodents cannot describe their distress. What does it mean for a committee — human or automated — to be confident in its assessment of an animal's suffering when the animal cannot confirm or deny it?
- Does the fact that an AI tool's distress predictions are trained on prior human-annotated behavioral data make those predictions more or less trustworthy than a human reviewer's judgment? What would make you more confident either way?

Week 3: Invertebrates and the Edges of the Moral Circle

Reading: Case packet B (cephalopod and insect protocols); selected readings on invertebrate nociception and behavioral complexity.
Discussion questions:
- Our institution currently requires IACUC review for cephalopods but not for most insects. Is this line defensible, arbitrary, or simply provisional pending better evidence?
- If an AI review tool flagged an insect protocol as ethically comparable in severity to a vertebrate protocol, would you want the committee to take that flag seriously, or would you consider it a miscalibration? What evidence would change your answer?

Week 4: Walkthrough — The AI-Assisted Flagging Module

No new reading. In-class guided exercise using the review software's flagging module on three retired, de-identified protocols.
Discussion questions:
- After seeing the tool generate its output, do you find its stated reasoning legible? Could you explain to an investigator why a given protocol was flagged, using the tool's own stated basis?
- The tool in our environment does not issue a yes/no verdict — it surfaces a predicted severity band, a note on reversibility, and a suggested alternative where one exists, then leaves the recommendation with the reviewer. Is this the right division of labor, or should the tool go further?

Week 5: Case Study — The Burrowing Protocol (required case; central to midterm)

Reading: Case packet C, in full.

This case will anchor much of our subsequent discussion, so read the full packet carefully, not just the abstract.

Summary: A doctoral candidate in behavioral neuroscience (in the case materials, referred to only as "the investigator," per our de-identification convention) submitted a protocol proposing a burrowing-suppression paradigm in laboratory mice to study anxiety-linked digging behavior, involving forty-one animals across three cohorts, with an estimated protocol duration of six weeks per cohort. The proposed method involved daily restriction of burrowing substrate access combined with mild acoustic startle exposure, on the premise that the resulting behavioral suppression would serve as a proxy measure for anxiety-like states, avoiding more invasive electrophysiological recording.
